local_names.romanized_local: Pazha Chaaru (Tamil romanized); Phalon ka Ras (Hindi romanized)

region_of_origin: Global / Middle East (served at Middle Eastern restaurant in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Fruit juices — fresh squeezed and from concentrate

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301710 covers fruit juice as a category. This record represents a menu offering of multiple juice varieties (fresh-squeezed and from concentrate); closest_parent used as no single code covers the full mixed offering.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fruit juices as a broad category. The specific mixed-variety menu offering (orange, lemon, lime, green apple fresh-squeezed; pineapple, mango, cranberry from concentrate) is not individually coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 covers fruit juice as a substance class; closest_parent used for the same reason as FoodOn.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately one glass (250 ml / 250 g) of fruit juice, which is the typical portion size served at a restaurant or café.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fruit juice, composite average (orange juice, lemon juice, lime juice, apple juice, pineapple juice, mango juice, cranberry juice); SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries used to derive a weighted representative mean for a mixed fresh-squeezed and from-concentrate fruit juice offering
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers this exact mixed offering. Values are derived as a representative mean across USDA SR Legacy entries for orange juice (FDC 169098), lemon juice (FDC 167747), lime juice (FDC 167748), apple juice (FDC 168869), pineapple juice (FDC 169124), mango nectar (FDC 169910), and cranberry juice cocktail (FDC 171697). The composite reflects a typical 100 g portion of a blended fruit juice category. Fresh-squeezed varieties are slightly lower in sugar than concentrate varieties; the composite represents a reasonable central estimate. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; fruit juice composite GI values including orange juice (GI ~50), apple juice (GI ~41), pineapple juice (GI ~46), mango juice (GI ~55), cranberry juice (GI ~52)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No single GI value exists for this exact mixed-variety juice menu offering. GI of 50 is a defensible central estimate derived from the University of Sydney GI Database values for the constituent juice types: orange juice ~50, apple juice ~41, pineapple juice ~46, mango juice ~55, cranberry juice cocktail ~52, lemon juice ~low (minimal carbohydrate contribution), lime juice ~low. The composite GI of 50 is consistent with the low-to-medium boundary and is conservatively classified as low (GI < 55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (50 × 11.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.75, rounded to 13.8.

hypertension.guidance: Fruit juices are naturally low in sodium (approximately 3 mg per 100 g) and provide potassium (~150 mg per 100 g), which supports blood pressure regulation; however, portion control is advised as large volumes contribute significant natural sugars and calories.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High natural sugar content and rapid glucose absorption due to absence of whole-fruit fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fruit juices, even fresh-squeezed, lack the dietary fibre of whole fruit and deliver concentrated natural sugars (~9.5 g sugar per 100 g) that can cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 100–125 ml and prefer whole fruit over juice where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat; vitamin C and potassium content beneficial
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fruit juices are essentially fat-free and saturated-fat-free, and provide vitamin C and potassium which are associated with cardiovascular benefit; however, regular high-volume consumption may contribute to excess caloric and sugar intake, which can adversely affect lipid profiles and body weight over time.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh and Concentrated Fruit Juices is a beverage category offered at Sahara Bar & Restaurant (a Middle Eastern restaurant at Boat Quay, Singapore) comprising two sub-categories: fresh-squeezed juices (orange, lemon, green lime, green apple) and juices from concentrate (pineapple, mango, cranberry). Fresh-squeezed juices are prepared by pressing whole fruit to order, yielding a beverage with bright, natural flavour, moderate sweetness, and high vitamin C content. Concentrate-based juices are reconstituted from commercially processed fruit concentrate and tend to be slightly sweeter and more uniform in flavour. All varieties are served chilled. Nutritionally, fruit juices provide readily available carbohydrates primarily as natural sugars (fructose, glucose, sucrose), with minimal fat and protein. They are a useful source of vitamin C, potassium, and folate, but lack the dietary fibre present in whole fruit. The glycemic index of the category is approximately 50 (low-to-medium boundary), but the glycemic load per standard 250 ml serving is moderate (~13.8), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. These juices are suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free diets. In the context of a Middle Eastern restaurant menu in Singapore, fresh juices are a common accompaniment to grilled meats, shawarma, and mezze platters, reflecting both Middle Eastern hospitality traditions and the multicultural beverage preferences of Singapore diners.
common_user_queries: What juices are available at Sahara Restaurant?
Is fresh orange juice available at Boat Quay?
Are the juices freshly squeezed or from concentrate?
What is the sugar content of fruit juice?
Is mango juice from concentrate healthy?
What is the GI of orange juice?
Is cranberry juice good for blood pressure?
Are these juices vegan and halal?
How many calories in a glass of fresh juice?

confidence_reason: No single approved source covers this exact mixed-variety juice menu offering. Nutrient values are estimated as a composite mean from multiple USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the constituent juice types. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for analogous individual juices. Confidence is medium rather than high because the record represents a category average rather than a laboratory-analysed specific product.
notes: This record represents a menu category of fruit juices served at Sahara Bar & Restaurant, Singapore (field survey listing). The offering includes both fresh-squeezed (orange, lemon, green lime, green apple) and from-concentrate (pineapple, mango, cranberry) varieties. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for each constituent juice type; no single source covers the full mixed offering. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fruit juices contain no vitamin B12 as it is found only in animal-derived foods; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Vitamin D is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fruit juices do not naturally contain vitamin D unless fortified; unfortified varieties confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). Saturated fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — fruit juices contain negligible saturated fat per USDA FoodData Central). Monounsaturated fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for fruit juices). Polyunsaturated fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for fruit juices). Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats in natural fruit juices). Cholesterol is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-derived beverages contain no cholesterol). GI set to 50 as a defensible composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for constituent juice types; classified as low (GI < 55). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (50 × 11.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.75, rounded to 13.8. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this food is served in Singapore. The cuisine tag is Middle Eastern (restaurant context) but the food itself is globally universal. Price SGD 7.63 preserved from input. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no single code covers the full mixed-variety offering.
